topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the sharp line of the ink with a hesitant fingertip. The canvas, usually a sanctuary of vibrant chaos, now felt cold beneath her touch. It was a depiction of a swirling nebula, breathtaking in its scope, yet something was terribly wrong. Hidden within the cosmic dust, a tiny, intricate symbol pulsed with a dark, almost predatory energy. Her breath hitched, catching in her throat like shattered glass. She leaned back, the wooden stool groaning in protest, and stared at the piece, her knuckles whitening where she gripped the edge of the easel. A tremor ran through her hands, making the brush tremble slightly in her other hand. Who had done this? The realization settled in her stomach like a stone, heavy and suffocating.

[story 2]

Marcus felt a prickle crawl up his neck as he finally noticed it. It wasn't a mistake in the composition; it was deliberate. A delicate, almost hidden script, woven into the shadows of his latest portrait. His heart began to hammer against his ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. He pulled his gaze away, focusing instead on the dusty windowpane, watching the streetlights smear across the glass. Every muscle in his body tightened; he wanted to bolt, to dismantle the entire studio, but his feet remained rooted. A cold sweat beaded on his forehead, slicking his skin. He whispered a soundless plea into the quiet room, his voice barely a rasp.

[story 3]

When Clara uncovered the secret, the air in the gallery seemed to thin out. The image she had spent weeks perfecting—a study in muted blues and deep violets—was marred by a stark, unsettling addition. It was a mark, clearly inked, placed with an unnerving precision over the central figure’s eye. Clara physically recoiled, stumbling backward until her spine hit the cool plaster of the wall. Her vision tunneled; the colors around her seemed to drain away, leaving only the stark, unsettling geometry of the intrusion. She swallowed hard, the dryness in her mouth absolute. Her gaze snapped back to the work, and she saw not art, but an undeniable, unwelcome claim.
